subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Uses standard IA structure with headings and includes key ESS terminology (PMâ.â, NOx, LEZ)
weaknesses: Organisation is uneven (repeated captions, misplaced text)
Formatting inconsistencies (bullets, figure numbering) and incomplete citations hinder clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication aligns with the 1â2 band: terminology is generally appropriate, but structure, formatting and referencing contain multiple errors. A score of 1 reflects the basic coherence of the report but significant lapses in presentation and citation style.

strengths: States a clear, relevant research question focused on LEZ and air quality
Provides a coherent urban airâpollution context linking policy to health and ecosystem impacts
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative local data (e.g., pollutant limits or exceedances) to substantiate context
Two versions of the research question create slight ambiguity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place Identifying the Context in the mid band (3â4). The submission states a focused question and outlines key pollutants and impacts but omits quantitative local evidence and contains minor inconsistencies in phrasing. A score of 3 reflects solid performance with room for deeper contextual detail.

strengths: Method is repeatable with clear steps (data sources, dates, software)
Sampling strategy described with logical site choices (inside, border, outside LEZ)
weaknesses: No justification for the chosen five-month window or pre-LEZ baseline
Risk and ethical considerations are noted but lack depth (no mitigation plan or calibration standards)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Planning strands are largely met at the 3â4 band: the method is appropriate and reproducible, and sampling is described. However, key justifications (timeframe rationale, statistical or logistical basis for site selection) are missing and risk discussion is cursory. A score of 3 reflects competent design needing stronger rationale.

strengths: Identifies basic methodological strengths (reliable secondary data, multiple sites)
weaknesses: Evaluation of conclusions is minimal and lacks environmental context
Improvement suggestions are superficial (longer data collection, generic primary data) with no specifics
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Discussion and evaluation align with the lower 1â2 band. While some strengths and weaknesses of the method are noted, the evaluation is brief and improvements are generic. A score of 1 reflects limited depth and specificity in critical reflection.

strengths: Constructs clear, multi-series line graphs with titles, axes and legends
Insightful discussion of the volcanic SOâ anomaly demonstrates contextual understanding
weaknesses: Analysis remains qualitative with no quantitative measures (means, % change, tests)
Missing summary tables, error bars and composite site comparisons to reinforce claims
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Results and analysis meet much of the 3â4 band: data are presented appropriately and trends are interpreted. The lack of quantitative analysis and comparative summaries limits the robustness of conclusions. A mid-band score of 3 reflects strong presentation but incomplete analytical depth.

strengths: Proposes relevant solutions (stricter emission standards, vehicle incentives) linked to investigation focus
weaknesses: Justification of applications is weak and general (âmy investigation suggestsâ¦â)
Strengths/weaknesses of proposed solutions are mentioned without thorough evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Applications meet elements of the band-1 descriptor: a potential solution is stated but justification and evaluation remain superficial. A score of 1 reflects the presence of an application but insufficient depth of justification or critical appraisal.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key ESS terminology (PM2.5, NOx, LEZ) is used accurately; however, several typographical errors and verbose sentences reduce concision.  The work therefore meets one of the two band-2 conditions (appropriate terminology) but not both for band-3.

The student comments that the LEZ âmay reduce pollution within the areaâ but causes rerouting effects, touching on environmental relevance.  Depth is limited; no consideration of socio-economic factors or alternative explanations is offered.  Thus the conclusion is described rather than fully evaluated, meriting 1-2 marks.

The conclusion restates observed trends and links them to the RQ, but support remains qualitative.  Some reference to data patterns is made (e.g., pollutants âlargely remained stable or slightly increasedâ) without quantitative backing.  This constitutes an interpretation with partial validity, appropriate to the 3-4 band but nearer the lower end.

Analysis is almost entirely qualitative: the student comments on âno significant decreaseâ, discusses wind/rain effects and a volcanic spike, but offers no calculations of mean values, percentage change or statistical tests.  These represent significant omissions even though the narrative is largely correct, placing the work in the upper end of the 1-2 band.

Only one improvement (longer data-collection period) and a very general suggestion to collect primary data are provided; no specific methodological refinements or extension questions are proposed.  The suggestions are therefore superficial, warranting the lower mark of the 1-2 band.

A step-by-step secondary-data method is provided that another investigator could repeat (named website, dates, pollutants, software for graphing).  The method will generate sufficient data to address the question, although no justification for the five-month window or any pre-LEZ baseline is given.  Hence the design is appropriate and repeatable but not fully optimised, suiting the upper part of the 3-4 band.

Connections between the issue (air pollution) and the RQ are described: LEZ is presented as a policy tool aimed at reducing the highlighted pollutants, and the student notes potential rerouting effects.  However, explanations remain qualitative and omit several pathways (e.g. baseline trends, seasonal factors).  The link is therefore described with some omissions rather than comprehensively explained.

The student states a clear question focused on the effect of the Edinburgh Low-Emission Zone on air quality.  The wording is slightly inconsistent (two versions appear and one widens the scope to âtransportation policiesâ), but the underlying focus remains the same pollutant-based comparison before/after the LEZ.  The question is relevant to the discipline and narrow enough to be investigated with the chosen secondary data, showing coherence even if perfect precision is lacking.

Some strengths (targets older vehicles) and weaknesses (economic impact on residents/businesses) of the proposed stricter standards are mentioned, but the discussion is not exhaustive.  A partial evaluation corresponds to 2 marks.

The report follows a recognisable IA structure with headings, figures and bibliography, but organisation is uneven (repetition of figure captions, misplaced text).  Structure is present but not sustained throughout, aligning with band 2.

Method strengths (reliable data, multiple sites) and weaknesses (secondary data, short time frame) are mentioned, but limitations such as lack of baseline or control of confounding variables are not explored in depth.  This identification of some strengths and weaknesses aligns with the 1-2 band.

The introduction outlines urban air pollution, identifies key pollutants (PM, NOx, SO2) and links these to health and ecosystem impacts.  The description is correct but brief; quantitative detail on local exceedances or legal limits is absent.  Overall the environmental issue is outlined sufficiently to give context but not explored in depth, fitting mid-band performance.

The choice of four sites (inside, border and outside the zone) is described and the student explains the logic in qualitative terms.  However, no statistical rationale (e.g. traffic counts, dominant wind directions, distance from boundary) is supplied.  This amounts to a description rather than a justification, matching the middle of the 3-4 band.

Risk and ethical issues are briefly outlined: equipment calibration, representativeness, and lack of human/animal involvement.  The points are valid but cursory, with no scoring matrix or mitigation actions.  This is an outline rather than a comprehensive description, warranting a mid-band score.

For each site the student provides multi-series line graphs with titles, axes, units and legends; a location map is also included.  However, summary tables, error bars, or comparative composite graphs are missing, and some images are low-resolution.  The work therefore fulfils âappropriate diagrams with some omissionsâ at the top of the 3-4 band.

The student proposes stricter emission standards and incentives for cleaner vehicles, explaining briefly how these could strengthen the LEZ.  The justification references findings only generally (âmy investigation suggestsâ¦â); it is present but weak, matching band 2.

The argument generally flows from context to method, results and conclusion, but frequent digressions (repeated figure explanations) and minor inconsistencies in the RQ hinder readability.  The presentation is mainly logical yet difficult to follow in parts, satisfying the descriptor for band-2.

annotations.comment: The introduction outlines the environmental issue but lacks quantitative detail on local pollutant exceedances or legal limits. Adding specific data (e.g., average PMâ.â levels) would strengthen the context.
Analysis describes trends qualitatively. Incorporate quantitative calculations (e.g., mean values, percentage changes) or basic statistical tests to support claims of âno significant decrease.â
Good identification of confounding factors like weather. Expand by referencing meteorological data or controlling for these variables in your analysis.
The volcanic SOâ spike analysis is insightful and well-researched. Comparing this anomaly to typical diurnal ranges further would clarify its impact on overall trends.
Conclusion restates findings but remains qualitative. Link directly to the research question with quantified results (e.g., % change in NOx) to fully support your claims.
Evaluation mentions secondary data and limited sites, but overlooks confounders like seasonal traffic patterns. Discuss how to mitigate these in future studies.
The research question is relevant and focused, but two versions appear causing slight ambiguity. Consolidate to one clear, precise wording aligned with the scope of pollutants and timeframe.
Include the IA month and year in the cover page only; its repetition in the header is unnecessary and distracts the reader.
Strong global-to-local context connects policy relevance effectively, demonstrating understanding of how LEZ measures fit broader urban sustainability efforts.
The introduction describes NOâ incorrectly as ânitrogen Î± dioxide.â Use correct chemical nomenclature $$\text{NO}_2$$ and ensure consistent terminology.
Provide quantitative local pollution data (e.g., legal limit exceedances, average concentrations) to substantiate the environmental context and show specific urban challenges.
Method description opening is vague. Reformulate as an aim statement separate from the research question and clearly define dependent and independent variables.
Choice of four sites is clearly described. To justify sampling strategy, include rationale based on traffic volumes or prevailing wind directions influencing pollutant dispersion.
Justify the MayâOctober 2024 period explicitly. Explain why a five-month window appropriately captures pre- and post-LEZ implementation air quality trends.
Ethical considerations are outlined but superficial. Expand by stating specific calibration checks or referencing equipment accuracy standards to mitigate data quality risks.
List item bullet formatting appears inconsistent (math bullets). Standardize bullet symbols and indentation for improved readability.
The map is helpful for spatial context. Consider adding a scale bar and north arrow for improved clarity and geospatial accuracy.
Figure 1 is well-labeled but resolution appears low. Ensure high-quality exports and consider adding error bars or confidence intervals to enhance reliability.
Graphs correctly include legends and axes but lack summary tables or composite comparisons across sites. Aggregate data in a single chart to illustrate overall LEZ impact.
Ensure consistency: figure numbering in captions does not always match in-text references. Verify caption numbers and citations for clarity.
Graph axes labels are clear, but units (Âµg/mÂ³) should be included directly on both axes for immediate clarity.
Captions are verbose and repetitive, focusing on method details rather than key findings. Concisely describe main trends to guide the reader.
The suggestion to extend data collection period is valid but superficial. Propose exact additional durations or new variables (e.g., winter months) to capture long-term LEZ effects.
The proposal of stricter emission standards is relevant but lacks strong justification. Link the recommendation directly to observed NOx and SOâ trends to support the solution.
The suggestion to apply measures in similar cities is insightful. Discuss potential socio-economic impacts and feasibility to enhance the application evaluation.
Bibliography uses Chicago style but entries are inconsistently formatted and URLs are truncated. Ensure references are complete, with uniform access dates.
The BBC citation lacks author and publication date details. Verify and complete all reference entries to improve credibility.
Good balance in method evaluation: strengths of reliable data sources and multiple sites are clearly identified.
